story:
Ernest Alwin, a blind American history professor, lingers at the library’s front door listening to sleet strike the glass, debating whether to brave the storm on foot or take the bus. Constance Peabody, the second assistant librarian, approaches to tell him the library is closing early because conditions are worsening. Grateful for her help—especially her promise to track down an Egypt-themed mystery novel in audio—Ernest decides the bus is the smarter option and heads out into the weather with his guide cane.

At the bus stop, the cold intensifies and the bus fails to arrive on time. When his watch beeps four o’clock, he realizes it’s dangerously late. Rather than stand freezing, he starts walking home, planning to flag the bus if he hears it. As he trudges along slick sidewalks and gravel-strewn edges of the park, he thinks about Constance: her steady competence, her jasmine scent in the cramped braille stacks, and the way her composed professionalism seems to mask insecurity. Earlier that day he’d even asked the cafeteria manager for a “profile” of her—height, hair, style—then bought two lattes and found her reading alone. Their small talk and her warmth toward him have left him increasingly certain she likes him too.

Meanwhile, Constance has noticed Ernest for a while. His broad shoulders, his quiet confidence, and the physical strength hinted at under a polo shirt have made an impression she tries not to acknowledge. As the storm worsens and news reports pile up with accidents and gridlock, she looks up Ernest’s address through the library’s customer system, worried about his trip home.

Out on the street, a car pulls alongside Ernest and honks. Constance leans over, opens the passenger door, and blurts out an offer to drive him. He happily accepts, tossing his backpack into the footwell. Inside the warm car, conversation shifts from logistics to connection. Constance asks about the French materials he’s been requesting; he explains he’s writing about the Franco-American alliance and struggling with 18th-century French. She reveals she double-majored in French and comparative literature and offers to help. The offer thrills him, and the talk turns personal as she shares how a teenage obsession with ancient Egypt led to ridicule and a cruel nickname—“Queen Nefertiti.”

When they reach his apartment building, Ernest asks her to call him Ernest, and he promises not to shorten her name to “Connie.” Their flirtation becomes intimate: he removes his glasses, senses her studying his damaged eyes, and feels her guide his hand to her cheek. He traces her jaw, brushes her mouth, and, choosing restraint, invites her to dinner Saturday instead of inside tonight. She agrees—seven o’clock—returns his forgotten glasses, and as he hesitates at the entryway, she comes back to seal the promise with a soft kiss to his ear: “Saturday at seven.”
